## [00:00 - 03:03]

Here's some really important real estate terms that you're going to be hit with when you're talking to brokers and bankers. Before we really unpack the mechanics of a deal, it's important that you know all the terms and that we're all on the same page so that when I'm saying X, you understand that doesn't mean something else. By the way, some of this language can change depending on where you go. I want to be sure we're speaking the same language and pushing toward the same collective goals. And this starts with key terms. Understand them. Know them. Know exactly what they mean when they say the gross rent income or the T12 or the T... There's a lot of terms here. Understand the terms and know how and when they apply and you'll be well positioned to present yourself like a smart, savvy real estate investor that you are and should be. Caps, expenditures, this relates to expenditures on those items not considered normal or recurring expenses to operate. For example, a new roof or an improvement of the clubhouse. These are not ongoing, everyday expenses, something by the way that you get to write off in that year. So you want to differentiate between cap X and ongoing expenses. Your lender by the way is going to ask you, hey what cap X do you have planned for your investment? Your investors are going to want to know down the road three years, four years, five years, seven years. Hey what money are we going to have to spend because you might have to dip into cash flow and or call more money from your investors to take care of a roof or a fence or major problems. Cap rates. This represents the rate of return the property will pay the investor if there is no debt on the property. Now, a high cap rate typically goes to a lower grade of property. A high cap rate is going to be properties that have more problems in worse locations that possibly possibly I say provide more cash flow. That doesn't mean they're going to appreciate that means there's more risk. The low cap rate, a three cap rate, a four cap rate, a four and a half or a five are going to be better properties in better locations. Remember cap rate is before you put debt on a property. So if I have an eight cap, we don't buy eight caps, very difficult to find them. If I buy an eight cap I would expect an eight percent return on my investment if I use no debt. Cap rate is calculated by the cash flow, free cash flow assuming no debt. So if a one million dollar property, let's take for example a one million dollar property provides me with a hundred thousand dollars worth of free cash flow and I paid cash, that's a ten cap. Let's talk about cash on cash. This is the calculation based on the cash invested only. This is calculated by dividing the free cash by the cash put down to purchase.

## [03:03 - 06:05]

So if I put two hundred and fifty thousand dollars down on my one million dollar investment and I make ten percent on my money, that would be twenty five thousand dollars of free cash or a ten percent cash on cash. Closing costs, these are the fees paid at the closing of a real estate transaction and can include everything from possibly an appraisal on the property, title on the property, fees to the bank or lender, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, could be fees to the broker on one side or both. These are closing costs and they should add to the price you paid for that property. Debt coverage ratio or DCR. This is a lender's term representing that your income from operation is greater than the debt from the bank. DCR is your debt coverage ratio. How much debt you have and will it cover, will it be covered by your NOI? Your NOI will have to be greater than your debt load. So if you take your full principal and interest, let's say it's a hundred thousand dollars a year, typically the bank is going to want to see one hundred and twenty five thousand dollars worth of DCR or debt coverage ratio. It's typically one fifteen to one twenty five. Down payment is the amount used to fund your acquisition less the debt. So if I'm buying a million dollar property, I'm putting two hundred fifty thousand dollars down, again this might seem simple, that two fifty represents your down payment. Economic occupancy which almost no one talks about. They talk about the physical occupancy but very rarely discuss economic occupancy. This is the percentage of the property's income that is actually collected. Some people talk about this as physical occupancy. You want to press for the economic occupancy, it will typically always be lower than the physical occupancy. Physical occupancy is the number of units divided into the total, the number of units rented or heads on beds divided into the total number. So if I have a hundred beds and ninety five of them are occupied, that is a ninety five percent physical occupancy but economic occupancy could be well lower than that because maybe I'm not getting paid by some of these people. The economic occupancy number is more valuable to me than the physical occupancy rate. Effective gross income, this is the amount of rental income actually collected and a very very important number. You're going to hear other numbers about pro forma income and our gross potential income but you want to look at your effective EGI, effective gross income. Expense ratio, this is your total operating expense divided by the gross operating income. Now I know I'm sharing some terms with you that you haven't heard yet but you have to get your head wrapped around these terms and know them exactly.

## [06:05 - 09:09]

Don't let anybody confuse you and by the way there's a lot of confused people out there including lenders, bankers and even brokers and listing agents. The expense ratio is the total operating expense divided into the gross operating income. That's your expense ratio. Now expenses, when you hear about expenses from normal operations of the property you're talking about the things that you have to pay for. Debt is not something you had to put on the deal but utilities you have to pay for, taxes you have to pay for. These are the kind of expenses a manager, a leasing manager, maintenance, lawn care, security, these are expenses that you cannot get away from. Basically it's those things that you have to do to keep the property going but not including again debt or major capital improvements like a new roof or clubhouse. Now the exit, you're going to hear me talk about the exit quite a bit. Not many people talk about the exit and I'm not sure why because that's where you make your money. The exit refers to how you as an owner are going to sell this property. Now if you're having your friends invest with you and you're giving them a piece of this deal the exit is vital. If you're getting a percentage from the investors to both manage to buy, to keep this property operating and then a percentage of the profit to sell, you have to know how to exit and it's important for your investors to know the same. This requires the ability to calculate what the next buyer can pay, can pay you in profit and still get a return on his investment because nobody's going to buy from you if they can't get the same thing you were looking for to start with. Lots of amateur investors miss this calculation, overlook it completely, cross their fingers and just think I'm going to make money but you have to know how do you exit? How can John buy the property for me? Pay himself and his investors and by the way get a loan on the deal. Lots of investors miss this calculation because they're only looking at the calculations of operations and never figure out how to exit. Gross operating income. To calculate your G.O.I. you'll need to look at the scheduled income and account for any losses from credits or vacancies plus income from other relevant sources.

## [09:09 - 12:11]

Gross operating income is less important to me because it's the biggest number less the credits. What I'm looking for is how much money did you actually collect? Your G.O.I. less your vacancy and credit loss plus your income derived from other sources will equal your gross operating income or gross potential income. Other sources of income by the way can include application fees, credit applications, pet fees, parking fees, late fees, garbage pickup, valet pickup, laundry, valet parking and our special parking VIP parking. You'll also have a sense of an average of vacancies and keep in mind that you're going to have some vacancy loss in every property. It's almost impossible to run a property at 100%. Even in a perfectly run building people will move out. There will be a turn almost always there will be a slight lag before your new tenant arrives. You can turn the property. Turn the property means get it ready for the new tenant. It could be a few days, a few weeks, it could be a few months. The faster you do it the more money you're going to make. So be sure you're hiring a manager that is hustling. Account for those days in your calculations. Over the course of a year a few days can add up to many months of lost income. Gross rental income. This is a total possible income if every unit is rented at full price. This figure is used by the broker and is almost meaningless. Now that being said I will take that number. I will slash it by 10%. That's my vacancy number in most markets. Cut it in half and know that that's going to be my NOI. So if you can do the math on what I just shared with you you're going to see how I actually pencil a deal out. I take the gross possible income if every unit was rented and I got the max rent and no concessions. I didn't give anything away to anyone. Write it down. Look at different performance. Look at different deals. The gross rent multiplier. There is a GRM for every market. Gross rent multiplier. You take the purchase price divided by the gross scheduled income and you're going to have your GRM. The lower this number is, the better. Means the better the return. It doesn't mean it's a better property though. GSI, you've heard me use this term over and over. You're like what is he saying in GSI? The gross scheduled income is calculated by the number of units times the market rent over 12 months with no vacancy. In other words, GSI is the annual rental income if the property could generate 100% occupied, 100% of the time with all rents collected and that will never happen. And if it does happen, your rents are too low.

## [12:11 - 15:16]

The way to do this calculation is take the units times the market rent times 12. That equals your annual GSI. Let me just say when I mention this word annual, do not do your calculations on a monthly basis. Do them annually. This property is not going to be flipped in two or three months. You're going to own it for years. Interest on a loan is money paid regularly or to an investor, money paid regularly at a particular rate for the use of the money borrowed. Interest should not be confused with principal pay down. You want to separate the principal amount pay down of pay down and separate the interest pay down. It's going to be a small number. This is a proportion of a loan charged as interest to the borrower to you, usually expressed as an annual percentage rate of the loan outstanding. Interest only. Now, interest only is an interesting vehicle because it allows the borrower to only pay interest on the loan and not pay the principal down. By the way, this is my preference. What I do on a property, if I'm buying a property of $30 million, I'm going to put probably 35% down, $10.5 million on that deal. I want three to five years of interest only. The reason I want that, I don't want to pay my principal down over those first five years is because I've already paid the loan down. This is not a house. When you put 3% down on a house, you're paying principal and interest in month one. When I put 35% or 40% down on an apartment deal, I have reduced the principal by 35% at purchase. I do not want to pay more principal down in the first three to five years because I want to inflate my cash flow. Interest only loans do exist in the market today. You have to have great credit. You have to have the right bank that you're talking to and you have to have a lender that loves multifamily and loves how they operate and provide security and safety long term as an investment. This is where, again, interest only, you only pay the interest on the amount borrowed. If you put enough money down at purchase, that was your principal pay down. Remember that. Put enough money down up front. You don't want to continue to pay your principal down in the first three to five years.
